title: GPU Basics
lesson:
A GPU (graphics processing unit) is the chip that makes modern AI possible. It was invented to draw video-game graphics, but it turned out to be perfect for the kind of math language models do. To build, fine-tune, or serve a model, you don't need to design a GPU — but you do need a working picture of why it's fast, where its limits are, and which numbers on the spec sheet actually matter. That's this lesson.

CPU vs GPU: a few geniuses vs a huge crowd

Your laptop's CPU (central processing unit — the general-purpose brain of a computer) is like a handful of brilliant chefs. Each one is fast, flexible, and can follow a complicated recipe with lots of decisions. But there are only a few of them. A GPU is the opposite: it's a stadium crowd of thousands of simpler workers, each not very clever on its own, but all doing the same small step at the same moment.

Here's the second analogy. Imagine you need to stamp the same address onto 100,000 envelopes. A CPU is one very skilled person stamping them one after another, quickly. A GPU hands a stamp to 10,000 people and they all press down at once. For one envelope, the skilled person wins. For 100,000 identical envelopes, the crowd finishes in a flash. AI is the 100,000-envelope job.

Why models love this

Almost everything a transformer does boils down to matrix multiplication — multiplying and adding huge grids of numbers (the model's parameters). A grid multiply is millions of tiny multiply-then-add steps that don't depend on each other, so they can all run in parallel. That's the exact shape of work a GPU's crowd is built for. This is why a model that crawls on a CPU can fly on a GPU — often 10x to 100x faster for the same job.

The four numbers that actually matter

When you read a GPU spec sheet or pick a cloud GPU, focus on these:

  • VRAM (video memory) — the GPU's own dedicated memory, where the model's weights and working data live. This is usually the make-or-break number; we cover it in depth in VRAM Basics. If a model doesn't fit in VRAM, it simply won't run (or runs painfully slowly).
  • Memory bandwidth — how fast data moves between VRAM and the compute cores, measured in gigabytes per second. For AI, this often matters more than raw math speed.
  • Compute — the number of math operations per second, measured in FLOPS (floating-point operations per second). More is better, but it's rarely the bottleneck for text generation.
  • Tensor cores — special units inside modern GPUs built only for matrix math at lower precision. They're the secret to fast AI and pair directly with quantization.

The hidden bottleneck: moving data, not doing math

Here's the surprise beginners miss. When a model generates one word at a time, the GPU's compute crowd is often idle, waiting for weights to arrive from VRAM. The math is cheap; fetching the numbers is slow. We call this being memory-bound: limited by data movement, not arithmetic. It's like a thousand cashiers standing ready but only one narrow door to bring stock from the warehouse. This single fact explains a huge amount of inference engineering — the KV Cache, Flash Attention, and batch inference all exist largely to keep that expensive crowd busy.

Checking your GPU

On a machine with an NVIDIA card, one command shows you everything that matters:

# Shows GPU model, VRAM total/used, temperature, and utilization
nvidia-smi

# Example line: NVIDIA RTX 4090 | 24576 MiB total | 18210 MiB used | 71% util

And from Python, to confirm your code actually sees the GPU:

import torch
print(torch.cuda.is_available())        # True if a usable GPU is found
print(torch.cuda.get_device_name(0))    # e.g. 'NVIDIA RTX 4090'
print(torch.cuda.mem_get_info())        # (free_bytes, total_bytes) of VRAM

The landscape in 2026

NVIDIA dominates AI, and the reason is as much software as silicon: CUDA (NVIDIA's programming toolkit) is what nearly every AI library is built on. Data-center cards like the H100 and newer B200 carry 80GB+ of fast VRAM and cost a fortune; consumer cards like the RTX 4090 (roughly 24GB) are great for hobby fine-tuning and local inference. Apple Silicon (M-series Macs) takes a different path with unified memory, where CPU and GPU share one pool — handy because that pool can be large, which is why MLX and Macs are popular for running models at home. The headline to keep: a GPU is a giant parallel crowd, and your first question for any model is always does it fit in VRAM, and can I feed the crowd fast enough?
mentalModel: A CPU is a few brilliant chefs working one task each; a GPU is a stadium crowd of thousands of simple workers all stamping the same step at once. AI is repetitive grid-math, so the crowd wins — but only if you can fit the work in VRAM and shovel data to them fast enough.
mistakes: Obsessing over compute (FLOPS) and ignoring VRAM. Beginners pick a GPU by its math-speed headline, then discover the model won't even load. For most AI work, VRAM capacity decides what you can run, and memory bandwidth decides how fast — see VRAM Basics. Raw FLOPS is usually the least binding limit for text generation.

Assuming a GPU makes everything faster. GPUs only win on big, parallel, repetitive work. A small model, a single short request, or code full of branchy decisions can be just as fast (or faster) on a CPU. The crowd only helps when there are 100,000 envelopes to stamp.

Forgetting that generation is memory-bound. People expect a beefier compute card to speed up one-word-at-a-time generation and are baffled when it barely helps. The cores sit idle waiting for weights to arrive from VRAM; the fix is higher bandwidth or smarter data reuse like the KV Cache, not more FLOPS.

Confusing system RAM with VRAM. '32GB of RAM' on your laptop is not GPU memory. The model needs to fit in the GPU's own VRAM (or in a Mac's shared unified memory). Mixing these up leads to wildly wrong sizing guesses.

Ignoring CUDA and the software stack. A capable AMD or unusual GPU can still be painful because most AI libraries assume NVIDIA's CUDA. Check that PyTorch actually reports cuda.is_available() == True before blaming the hardware; a silent fallback to CPU is a common 'why is this so slow?' trap.

Renting the biggest cloud GPU 'to be safe'. An 80GB H100 for a 7B model that fits on a 24GB card just burns money. Size the GPU to the job — quantize first, measure VRAM, then pick. See Cloud GPUs and quantization.
exercise:
Find any GPU you can use — your own machine, a free cloud notebook (Colab, Kaggle), or a rented cloud GPU. Run nvidia-smi and write down three numbers: the GPU model, total VRAM, and current utilization. Then in Python, run the three torch.cuda lines from the lesson and confirm your code actually sees the GPU. Finally, look up your card's memory bandwidth (GB/s) and its VRAM (GB) and jot both down.

Stretch goal: Load a small model (say a 1–3B parameter model from Hugging Face) and run the same short prompt once on CPU and once on the GPU, timing each. Then watch nvidia-smi while generating a long response and notice whether GPU utilization is pinned near 100% or bouncing low — low utilization during single-request generation is the memory-bound effect from the lesson, live in front of you.
solution: Expect torch.cuda.is_available() to print True and the device name to match what nvidia-smi showed; if it prints False, your PyTorch build or drivers don't match the GPU and you'd silently be running on CPU. In the timing test, the GPU should crush the CPU on a model of real size — often 10x or more — but the gap may be small for a tiny model or a one-word reply, which is exactly the point: GPUs win on big parallel work. Watching nvidia-smi during single-request generation, you'll likely see utilization sitting well below 100%; the cores are waiting on VRAM, proving generation is memory-bound. Batching several requests at once (or using a serving engine like vLLM) is what finally pushes utilization up.
summary: A GPU is a massively parallel chip — a huge crowd of simple workers all doing the same step at once — which fits the grid-math (matrix multiplication) at the heart of every transformer. When sizing one, VRAM and memory bandwidth usually matter more than raw compute, because text generation is memory-bound: the cores often wait on data. NVIDIA plus CUDA dominates as of 2026, with Apple's unified-memory Macs a popular home alternative. First question for any model: does it fit in VRAM, and can I feed the crowd fast enough?

keyTerms.def: A chip with thousands of simple cores that all run the same operation in parallel. Built for graphics, it turned out to be ideal for the grid-math AI models do.
A computer's general-purpose brain — a few very fast, flexible cores. Great at complex, decision-heavy tasks; slow at huge batches of identical math.
The GPU's own dedicated, fast memory where model weights and working data live. If a model doesn't fit in VRAM, it won't run normally.
How fast data moves between VRAM and the compute cores, in gigabytes per second. For AI generation this is often the real speed limit.
Floating-point operations per second — a measure of raw math throughput. Higher is better, but it's rarely the binding limit for text generation.
When a task's speed is capped by how fast data can be moved, not by how fast math is done. One-word-at-a-time generation is usually memory-bound.
Specialized units inside modern GPUs that do matrix math at lower precision very fast — the engine behind quick, quantized AI.
NVIDIA's software toolkit for programming its GPUs. Nearly every AI library is built on it, which is a big reason NVIDIA dominates.
An architecture (used by Apple Silicon Macs) where CPU and GPU share one memory pool instead of separate RAM and VRAM, letting models use a large shared space.
